mappings.rationaleAI: Directly addresses the boundary between scientific inquiry and religious belief, examining Gould's non-overlapping magisteria model and challenges from evolutionary biology, cosmology, and neuroscience to religious claims (G.2).

Examines the soul as an immaterial essence across philosophy and theology, engaging questions about the existence of the divine (A.1.a), divine attributes (A.1.b), immortality (A.1.d), whether the divine is personal or impersonal (A.2), and religious doctrines of moral judgment after death (D.3).

Examines prayer as spiritual communication with the divine across religious traditions, covering religious experience (B), basic patterns of religious action (C), and the tension between petition and contemplation central to mystical theology (D.2).

mappings.episodeTitle: William James's 'The Varieties of Religious Experience'
mappings.rationaleAI: James's empirical study of conversion, mysticism, saintliness, and prayer as personal psychological experience founded the psychology of religion, directly addressing religious experience (B), mystical theology (D.2), and systematic classification of religious phenomena (E).

Gnostic dualistic cosmology dividing existence into flawed material creation and transcendent divine reality exemplifies religious dualism (D.3.b) and doctrines of salvation through gnosis (D.8), while the Nag Hammadi discoveries transformed scholarly classification of early religious movements (E).

Aquinas's synthesis of Aristotelian philosophy with Christian doctrine produced arguments for God's existence (A.1.a), explored the scope of human knowledge of the divine through reason and revelation (A.1.c, D.1.b), articulated classical theism (D.3.c.i), and grounded ethics in natural law accessible to reason (G.4).

The dispute between Pelagius and Augustine over original sin, innate moral capacity, and the necessity of divine grace directly addresses the problems of free will (A.1.d) and doctrines of grace and salvation (D.8) that permanently shaped Western Christian theology.

The Genesis narrative of Adam and Eve's banishment is foundational to Christian doctrines of original sin and salvation (D.8), creation theology (D.4), the role of Sacred Scripture (D.1.a), and shaped Western conceptions of moral responsibility and human nature (G.4).

Plotinus's metaphysical system of emanation from the One, linking human souls with the divine through contemplation and ascent, exemplifies pantheistic and panentheistic doctrines (D.3.c.iii) and mystical theology (D.2), and profoundly influenced Christian, Jewish, and Islamic theology on questions of divine personality (A.2).

Traces how moral concepts evolved from religious frameworks through Enlightenment rationalism, engaging the problem of evil and suffering (A.1.d) and whether religion remains necessary for moral judgment (G.4), including Nietzsche's critique of Christianity and Darwin's challenge to divine command ethics.

Examines whether Christianity invented evil as a moral category, Nietzsche's critique of Christian 'herd morality,' and the philosophical history of evil from ancient thought through medieval Christianity — directly addressing the problem of evil (A.1.d) and religion's relationship to morality (G.4).

Charts the spread of religious doubt through Enlightenment rationalism, scientific discovery, and philosophical critique, covering deism, agnosticism, and atheism (D.3.d), challenges to the existence of God (A.1.a), and the conflict between religion and science (G.2).

The doctrine of karma as moral causation governing rebirth addresses free will versus determinism (A.1.d), divine governance and providence through karmic law (D.6), and paths to liberation as soteriological doctrines (D.8) across Hindu, Jain, and Buddhist traditions.

Hindu cosmogonic traditions from the Rig Veda's Nasadiya Sukta through cyclical creation-dissolution directly address doctrines of creation (D.4), encompassing both polytheistic (D.3.a) and monotheistic frameworks (D.3.c), and illustrating Hinduism's pluralistic theological character.

Mithraic mystery religion with its initiation grades, communal meals, and underground temples exemplifies basic patterns of religious action and association (C), while scholarly debate over its Iranian origins and relationship to Christianity illuminates the study and classification of religions (E).

Free will as a philosophical and theological problem debated for over two millennia directly addresses the special problem of free will (A.1.d), spanning determinism, Spinoza's theological framework, and the implications for moral responsibility.

Julian's Revelations of Divine Love records direct mystical visions of Christ (B, D.2), while her theology of divine love, the feminine aspects of God, and the nature of sin develops substantive doctrines concerning God within Christian monotheism (D.3.c).

Kierkegaard's exploration of the leap of faith, despair, and the religious stage of existence directly engages basic questions in philosophy of religion (A.1), religious experience through subjective inwardness (B), and critique of institutional Christianity that anticipates existentialist approaches to atheism and agnosticism (D.3.d).

Christianity's elevation of hope to a theological virtue alongside faith and charity is a doctrinal development concerning God and salvation (D.3), while the contrast between Greek suspicion of hope and Christian valorization illuminates religion's relationship to morality (G.4).

The Muses as goddesses associated with natural features and creative inspiration exemplify nature worship (F.8), while their cult raising questions about whether artistic creation channels divine gift directly addresses religion and art (G.1).

Akhenaten's elevation of the solar god Aten above the traditional polytheistic pantheon represents a historical transition from polytheism (D.3.a) toward monotheistic impulses (D.3.c), providing important evidence for the study and classification of religions (E).

Shinto's concept of kami as sacred presences in natural features exemplifies nature worship (F.8) and questions about whether the divine is personal or impersonal (A.2), while its ritual practices illustrate basic patterns of religious action (C) and its syncretism with Buddhism is significant for the classification of religions (E).

Boethius's examination of divine providence, free will, and theodicy in the Consolation of Philosophy directly addresses the special problems of free will, evil and suffering (A.1.d) and doctrines of divine government and providence (D.6), bridging classical thought and Christian theology.

Traces guilt through Christian doctrines of sin and conscience, exploring how religious concepts of moral responsibility shaped Western ethics and psychology — directly relevant to the relationship between religion and morality (G.4).

Traces conceptions of space from medieval theology through modern physics, examining how religious metaphysics and scientific cosmology have shaped understanding of spatial reality — addressing doctrines of creation (D.4) and the relationship between religion and science (G.2).

Examines how the concept of duty connects moral philosophy with religion, tracing the religious and theological roots of obligation and moral claims on human action — relevant to religion and morality (G.4).

Compares the claims of philosophy, science, the arts, and religion as competing paths to self-knowledge and human flourishing, directly engaging basic questions about what religion offers for understanding human existence (A.1) and the intersection of religion and morality (G.4).

Philosophical materialism's denial of spirit and divine agency, from ancient atomism through d'Holbach's condemnation for denying God, directly challenges the existence of the divine (A.1.a) and exemplifies the philosophical basis for atheism and agnosticism (D.3.d).
The episode directly addresses eschatological theories about the future age (D.7) and doctrines of grace and salvation (D.8) across Christian traditions.

Kali's dual nature as fierce destroyer and nurturing mother engages questions about whether the divine is personal or impersonal (A.2.a), one or more beings (A.2.b), basic patterns of religious thought (C), and the role of myth in religion (F.1).

The comparative treatment of prophetic traditions across three Abrahamic faiths connects to the general knowledge and understanding of religion, including how revelatory experience functions across traditions.

The novel's rich allegorical dimensions encompass Ahab's monomaniacal pursuit as quasi-religious quest, the white whale as symbol of nature's indifference or divine inscrutability, connecting to the knowledge and understanding of religious experience and symbolism.

Zoroastrian monotheism centered on truth as supreme tenet (D.3.c.i theism), its eschatological doctrines of judgment, heaven, hell, and final renovation (D.7), doctrines of grace and salvation through moral choice (D.8), and its cosmological dualism between truth and falsehood (D.3.b religious dualism).

Miracles as reported supernatural events are the direct subject of D.11; Hume's empiricist critique concerns the extent to which mankind can have knowledge of the divine (A.1.c); the science-religion debate around miracles connects to religion and science (G.2).

The concept of redemption across theology and philosophy, and contemporary debates about whether redemption retains meaning without theological foundations, relate to diverse understandings of religion and the philosophy of religion (A.2, A.3).

Apocalyptic thinking's contribution to Western conceptions of linear historical progress, divine judgment, and millenarianism relates to the understanding and interpretation of religious phenomena (A.2) and the philosophy of religion (A.3).
The attempt to prove God's existence through reason alone and Kant's denial that existence is a predicate relate to the philosophy of religion and rational theology (A.3).

Victorian anxieties about the existence of God after Darwin and biblical criticism relate to fundamental questions about the existence of the divine (A.1.a) and the extent to which mankind can have knowledge of the divine (A.1.c)

The reinterpretation of folk practices as satanic threats raises fundamental questions about evil and suffering in religious understanding (A.1.d); the patterns of religious thought and action involved in persecution and demonology relate to religious phenomenology (C)

Yeats's mystical pursuits - automatic writing, seances, and the Golden Dawn's ceremonial magic - represent varieties of religious experience including visionary and ecstatic states (B religious experience)
